title: The Binomial Distribution
blurb: Repeated independent trials: binomial probabilities, expected count np, shape, and simulation.
buildsOn: Combinations (Unit 3) and random variables (Unit 7) — the course's two halves meet here.

lessons.title: The Yes/No Atom
lessons.concreteComparison: A free throw goes in or it doesn't. A part is defective or it isn't. An email gets opened or ignored. Strip away the details and each is the same tiny machine: ONE trial, TWO outcomes, a fixed success probability $p$. Stack $n$ identical, independent copies of that machine and you get the setting behind half of applied statistics — but first, learn to recognize the atom.
lessons.objective: Model yes/no experiments as Bernoulli trials and verify the four binomial-setting conditions before using any formula.
concept: A **Bernoulli trial** is one experiment with exactly two outcomes — success (probability $p$) and failure (probability $1 - p$). "Success" is just the outcome you're counting; a defective part can be the "success".
The **binomial setting** stacks trials under four conditions — check them as **BINS**: **B**inary outcomes each trial; **I**ndependent trials; **N** fixed in advance; **S**ame $p$ every trial.
The count of successes $X$ in a binomial setting is a random variable (Unit 7!) taking values $0, 1, \ldots, n$ — its distribution is this unit's star.

Condition-checking is real work, not ritual: drawing cards WITHOUT replacement breaks I and S (the deck changes); "flip until you get heads" breaks N (no fixed trial count); a survey question with five choices breaks B until you binarize it ("chose A" vs "didn't").
lessons.keyIdea: Bernoulli trial = one yes/no with probability p; binomial setting = BINS (binary, independent, fixed n, same p) — verify before you compute.

facts.title: The atom
facts.explanation: One trial, two outcomes; 'success' is whatever you're counting.

facts.title: BINS
facts.explanation: The four conditions that make the binomial machinery legal.

workedExamples.statement: A basketball player hits free throws with $p = 0.8$, and attempts exactly $10$ in practice. Check BINS for $X = $ number of makes.
workedExamples.solution: Binary: make/miss ✓. Independent: reasonable for practice throws ✓ (a modeling assumption — note it). N: fixed at $10$ ✓. Same $p$: $0.8$ throughout ✓ (assumes no fatigue). Binomial setting holds; $X$ takes values $0$–$10$.

workedExamples.statement: Five cards are dealt WITHOUT replacement; $X = $ number of hearts. Is this binomial?
workedExamples.solution: Binary ✓ (heart or not), N fixed ✓ — but each card CHANGES the deck: trials are dependent and $p$ drifts ($\frac{13}{52}$, then $\frac{12}{51}$ or $\frac{13}{51}$...). I and S both fail. Not binomial — it's the without-replacement world of Units 5–6 (chooses and trees handle it).

workedExamples.statement: A factory line produces parts with a $3\%$ defect rate; an inspector samples $20$ parts from a huge batch. Model $X = $ defect count.
workedExamples.solution: "Success" $=$ defective, $p = 0.03$. Binary ✓; N $= 20$ ✓; same $p$ ✓; independence — technically sampling without replacement, but from a HUGE batch the deck barely changes: independence holds approximately (the standard modeling call when the sample is under $\sim 10\%$ of the population). Binomial with $n = 20$, $p = 0.03$.

commonMistakes.text: Applying binomial formulas to without-replacement draws from small pools.
commonMistakes.correction: Five cards from one deck: p changes every draw — use chooses (Unit 5), not binomial. The big-batch exception (sample < 10% of population) is a stated approximation, not a free pass.
Success is the counted outcome — often a defect, a miss, or a no-show. Fix p to YOUR counted outcome before anything else.

tryIt.statement: A die is rolled $12$ times; $X = $ number of sixes. Verify BINS and state $n$ and $p$.
tryIt.solution: Binary (six/not) ✓, independent rolls ✓, $n = 12$ fixed ✓, $p = \frac{1}{6}$ constant ✓. Binomial: $n = 12$, $p = \frac{1}{6}$.

tryIt.statement: "Shoot free throws until you miss" — which BINS letter fails?
tryIt.solution: N: the number of trials isn't fixed in advance (it depends on the results). Not binomial.

steps.title: One trial, two outcomes
beats: Free throw: in or out. Part: defective or fine. Email: opened or not.
Same machine every time: success $p$, failure $1 - p$.
"Success" = the outcome you COUNT. It can be the bad one.
This atom, stacked, powers half of applied statistics.

steps.title: Check BINS before anything
beats: Binary — two outcomes per trial.
Independent — trials don't influence each other.
N — trial count fixed BEFORE the experiment.
Same p — the probability never drifts. All four, or no binomial.

steps.title: One trial, repeated forever
steps.teach: A Bernoulli trial is a single yes/no with a fixed $p$ — here, a free throw that goes in with probability $\frac{3}{10}$. One shot tells you almost nothing. But shoot a few hundred and the make-rate settles onto the dashed line: the fixed $p$ is real, and repetition is how it shows itself. The binomial distribution is built out of exactly these repeats.

steps.title: Spot the break
steps.prompt: "Draw 4 marbles one at a time from a bag of 10, no replacement; count the reds." Which condition(s) fail?
options: Independence and same-p — the bag changes
steps.explanation: Each draw reshapes the bag: the next red-probability depends on what left. From a bag of 10, the drift is huge — chooses and trees are the right tools. (From 10,000 marbles, the drift would be negligible and binomial would approximate well.)

steps.title: What's p here?
steps.prompt: An airline's no-show rate is $6\%$; $X = $ no-shows among $200$ booked passengers. The trial's "success" and $p$ are…
steps.explanation: You're COUNTING no-shows, so that's the success, with its own probability $0.06$. Mislabeling p as 0.94 flips every formula downstream — fix the semantics first.

steps.title: Independence is a claim about the world
steps.body: Free throws, coin flips, well-mixed samples: independence is reasonable. Exam questions answered by one student, consecutive rainy days, opinions within one family: correlated — binomial will understate the spread. Say the assumption out loud when you make it; statisticians get paid to notice when it's false.

steps.title: Bernoulli's 20-year book
steps.body: Jacob Bernoulli worked on Ars Conjectandi — the book that formalized these trials and proved the law of large numbers — for twenty years, and it was still unpublished when he died in 1705. His nephew finished it. The 'atom of chance' took a lifetime to pin down; you get it in one lesson.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Bernoulli trial: one yes/no with success probability p (success = what you count).
Binomial setting = BINS, verified — not assumed.
Without-replacement from small pools is NOT binomial; huge pools approximately are.

lessons.title: The Binomial Formula
lessons.concreteComparison: What's the chance of EXACTLY $2$ made free throws in $5$ attempts at $p = 0.8$? One specific script — make, make, miss, miss, miss — has probability $0.8^2 \times 0.2^3$. But the two makes could sit in ANY $2$ of the $5$ slots: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ scripts, all equally likely. Total: $10 \times 0.8^2 \times 0.2^3 = 0.0512$. Unit 3's chooses count the scripts; Unit 6's independence prices each one.
lessons.objective: Derive and apply P(X = k) = C(n,k) p^k (1−p)^(n−k), understanding each factor's job.
concept: **The binomial formula:** $$P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} \, p^k \, (1-p)^{n-k}$$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n$.

Each factor has one job: $p^k$ prices the $k$ successes (independence multiplies), $(1-p)^{n-k}$ prices the failures, and $\binom{n}{k}$ counts WHICH trials succeeded — every arrangement of the script is a distinct, equally likely outcome.

The formula is literally the binomial THEOREM's term (Unit 4): expand $(p + q)^n$ with $q = 1 - p$ and the $k$-th term IS $P(X = k)$. Since $p + q = 1$, the whole expansion sums to $1^n = 1$ — the distribution audit, prepaid.
Edge cases sanity-check everything: $P(X = n) = p^n$ (all succeed — one script, no choose needed since $\binom{n}{n} = 1$) and $P(X = 0) = (1-p)^n$ — the complement workhorse.
lessons.keyIdea: P(X=k) = C(n,k) p^k (1−p)^(n−k): the choose counts the scripts, the powers price one script.

facts.title: The binomial formula
facts.explanation: Scripts counted by the choose; each script priced by independence.

facts.title: Sums to 1 by the theorem
facts.explanation: Unit 4's binomial theorem IS the distribution audit.

workedExamples.statement: Five free throws at $p = 0.8$: find $P(\text{exactly } 2 \text{ makes})$.
workedExamples.solution: Scripts: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$. Each script: $0.8^2 \times 0.2^3 = 0.64 \times 0.008 = 0.00512$. Total: $10 \times 0.00512 = 0.0512$ — about a $5\%$ chance; two makes is a bad day for an $80\%$ shooter.

workedExamples.statement: A fair coin is flipped $6$ times. Find $P(\text{exactly } 3 \text{ heads})$.
workedExamples.solution: $\binom{6}{3} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 20 \times \frac{1}{64} = \frac{20}{64} = \frac{5}{16} \approx 0.31$. Even the MOST likely count happens under a third of the time — exact counts are rarer than intuition says.

workedExamples.statement: A die is rolled $4$ times. Find $P(\text{exactly one six})$.

commonMistakes.correction: p^k(1−p)^(n−k) prices ONE script. Exactly-k can happen via C(n,k) different scripts — forgetting the count underestimates by a factor of up to hundreds.
The two exponents must sum to n: k successes AND n−k failures. P(2 makes in 5) has 0.8² × 0.2³ — a 0.8² × 0.2² is pricing a 4-trial script for a 5-trial question.

tryIt.statement: Three flips of a fair coin: use the formula for $P(\text{exactly } 2 \text{ heads})$ and check against the listed universe.
tryIt.solution: $\binom{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{3}{8}$. Universe check: HHT, HTH, THH — three of eight ✓.

tryIt.statement: A quiz has $5$ questions with $4$ options each; a student guesses all. Find $P(\text{exactly } 0 \text{ correct})$.

steps.title: Price a script, count the scripts
beats: One script for 2-of-5 makes: M M X X X → $0.8^2 \times 0.2^3$.
Every script with two M's costs the SAME (multiplication commutes).
How many scripts? Which 2 of 5 slots hold the M's: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$.
$P = 10 \times 0.8^2 \times 0.2^3$. Unit 3 counts; Unit 6 prices.

steps.title: Three factors, three jobs
beats: $\binom{n}{k}$: which trials succeeded. $p^k$: the successes' price. $(1-p)^{n-k}$: the failures'.
Exponents sum to $n$ — every trial appears exactly once.
And the whole table sums to $(p + (1-p))^n = 1$: Unit 4's theorem, auditing.

steps.title: The formula, one bar at a time
steps.teach: Six fair coin flips. Each bar is one value of the formula $P(X=k) = \binom{6}{k} p^k (1-p)^{6-k}$ — tap $k = 3$ and read it: 20 orderings, each with probability $(1/2)^6$, giving $\frac{20}{64}$. The $\binom{n}{k}$ out front is the counting of Act One doing its job inside a probability.

steps.title: Why times ten?
steps.explanation: Ten distinct scripts (MMXXX, MXMXX, ...) each carry probability $0.8^2 0.2^3$; the event collects them all. The choose factor IS Unit 3 cashing in.

steps.title: Assemble it
steps.explanation: Three successes at $0.3$ each, five failures at $0.7$, times the $\binom{8}{3} = 56$ patterns. Swapped exponents price the wrong script; the bare powers price only ONE script.

steps.title: Check k = 0 and k = n first
steps.body: Before trusting any binomial table you build, verify the ends: P(X=0) = (1−p)^n and P(X=n) = p^n — no choose factor needed (there's one script each). If your formula's ends don't match these, an exponent or a choose is misplaced.

steps.title: The formula that grades A/B tests
steps.body: Every 'version B got 54 clicks out of 100 vs version A's 47' decision at every tech company runs on binomial probabilities: could a fair 50/50 split produce a gap this size by luck? The formula you just learned — plus a threshold — is the entire skeleton of online experimentation.

steps.title: What you learned
points: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^(n−k): count scripts, price one, multiply.
Exponents sum to n; the table sums to 1 via the binomial theorem.

lessons.title: Cumulative Questions & Complements
lessons.concreteComparison: An airline sells $52$ seats on a $50$-seat plane, knowing each passenger shows up with $p = 0.95$. Overbooking fails only if MORE than $50$ show — that's $P(X = 51) + P(X = 52)$: two formula terms, not fifty-two. Real binomial questions are rarely "exactly k"; they're at-least, at-most, and between — and the art is choosing which terms to compute.
lessons.objective: Answer at-least/at-most/between binomial questions efficiently, choosing between direct terms and the complement.
concept: Cumulative events are SUMS of exact terms: $P(X \le 2) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2)$; $P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(0)$ — the complement reflex from Unit 5, now saving five terms at a time.

The term-count rule: compute whichever side has FEWER terms. "At least 1 of 20" → complement (one term); "at most 2 of 20" → direct (three terms); "more than 50 of 52" → direct from the top (two terms).
"Between" means reading carefully: $P(3 \le X \le 5)$ is three terms; strict "between 3 and 5" is just $P(4)$. Inclusive/exclusive kills more points than the formula ever does.

Layered problems chain units: FIRST binomial-count the successes, THEN use the count downstream (does the plane overflow? does the batch pass inspection?). The binomial is a component, not always the finale.
lessons.keyIdea: Cumulative = sum of exact terms; compute the smaller side, reach for 1 − P(none) on every 'at least one', and read inclusive/exclusive twice.

facts.title: The complement workhorse
facts.explanation: One term beats n terms — the single most-used binomial identity.

facts.title: Term-count rule
facts.explanation: At-least from the top or complement from the bottom — count before computing.

workedExamples.statement: A die is rolled $5$ times. Find $P(\text{at least one six})$.
workedExamples.solution: Complement: $P(X \ge 1) = 1 - \left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^5 = 1 - \frac{3125}{7776} = \frac{4651}{7776} \approx 0.598$. One term. (Direct would need five.)

workedExamples.statement: A batch passes inspection if a $10$-part sample contains AT MOST $1$ defective. With true defect rate $p = 0.1$, find $P(\text{pass})$.
workedExamples.solution: Two terms: $P(0) = 0.9^{10} \approx 0.349$; $P(1) = \binom{10}{1}(0.1)(0.9)^9 \approx 0.387$. $P(\text{pass}) \approx 0.736$ — a $10\%$-defective process still passes this gate nearly three times in four. (Inspection design is a real trade-off.)

workedExamples.statement: The airline: $52$ tickets, $50$ seats, show-up $p = 0.95$. Which terms give $P(\text{overbooked})$, and what's the expected number of shows?
workedExamples.solution: Overbooked $= P(X = 51) + P(X = 52) = \binom{52}{51}(0.95)^{51}(0.05) + (0.95)^{52}$ — two terms from the top. Expected shows (Unit 7 linearity): $52 \times 0.95 = 49.4$ — under capacity on average, which is exactly why airlines dare.

commonMistakes.text: Complementing 'at least one' as 'at least one failure'.
commonMistakes.correction: The complement of X ≥ 1 is X = 0 — no successes at all: (1−p)^n. Writing 1 − p^n instead computes the complement of 'ALL succeed', a different event.
'More than 50' starts at 51; 'at least 50' includes 50. One word, one term, and in overbooking that term is the whole answer.

tryIt.statement: Four flips of a fair coin: find $P(\text{at least one head})$ and $P(\text{at most one head})$.

steps.title: Nobody asks 'exactly'
beats: Airlines ask: more than 50 shows? Inspectors: at most 1 defect?
Cumulative events = SUMS of exact-k terms.
The art is choosing WHICH terms — never all of them.
Term-count rule: compute the smaller side.

steps.title: Steer n and p
steps.teach: This is $X \sim B(5, 0.3)$ — five tries at a 30% skill. Step $p$ up and watch the pile slide right; step $n$ up and watch it stretch. Every real binomial problem starts exactly here: identify $n$, identify $p$, and the whole distribution follows.

steps.title: Count the terms
steps.explanation: The complement of $X \ge 2$ is $X \le 1$: two cheap terms. Fourteen direct terms compute the same number the long way; $1 - P(15)$ answers 'not all', a different question.

steps.title: Inclusive or not?
steps.explanation: "Fewer than 3" excludes 3: $X \le 2$. "At most 3" would include it. One word, one term — read the inequality twice before summing.

steps.title: Write the term list before any arithmetic
steps.body: First line of every cumulative problem: the event as an explicit term list — 'P(X ≥ 2) = 1 − P(0) − P(1)'. Only then compute. Term-selection errors are invisible inside arithmetic but obvious in a list.

steps.title: Overbooking is a solved equation
steps.body: Airlines set ticket limits by inverting exactly the overbooking computation: find the largest n with P(shows > seats) below their tolerance (often ~1–5%). Too timid leaves seats empty; too bold buys hotel vouchers. The two-term tail you computed is a revenue-management department's entire job, iterated.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Cumulative = sums of exact terms; compute the smaller side.
'More than' vs 'at least': one word, one term, read twice.

lessons.title: Mean, Spread & Shape
lessons.concreteComparison: One free throw at $p = 0.8$ is a tiny distribution with mean $0.8$. Ten independent throws? Unit 7's linearity adds ten copies: $E(X) = 10 \times 0.8 = 8$ makes — no formula-grinding, no table. The binomial's mean, spread, and even its SHAPE follow from its anatomy as a sum of atoms.
lessons.objective: Use E(X) = np and Var(X) = np(1−p), and predict a binomial's shape (symmetric vs skewed) from p.
concept: **Mean:** $E(X) = np$ — the count is a sum of $n$ indicators, each with expectation $p$ (Unit 7's linearity, verbatim). Ten throws at $0.8$: expect $8$.

**Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p)$, so $\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}$ — independence lets the atoms' variances add (each atom: $p(1-p)$). Spread is largest at $p = \frac{1}{2}$ and shrinks toward certainty at either extreme.

**Shape:** $p = \frac{1}{2}$ gives a symmetric hill (Pascal's row, literally — Unit 4). $p < \frac{1}{2}$ piles probability on LOW counts (right/positive skew); $p > \frac{1}{2}$ mirrors it. Growing $n$ smooths any $p$'s hill toward the bell curve (Act Three's normal).

The **usual-range rule of thumb**: outcomes within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ are unremarkable; outside that range deserves a raised eyebrow (formalized next lesson). Ten throws at $0.8$: $\mu = 8$, $\sigma \approx 1.26$ — so $5$ makes is edge-of-normal, $3$ makes is genuinely surprising.
lessons.keyIdea: E = np, Var = np(1−p): atoms add. Shape follows p (symmetric at ½, skewed toward the rare side), and μ ± 2σ frames what's 'usual'.

facts.title: Binomial moments
facts.explanation: Linearity sums the atoms' means; independence sums their variances.

facts.title: The usual range
facts.explanation: The rule-of-thumb window for unremarkable outcomes.

workedExamples.statement: Ten free throws at $p = 0.8$: find the mean, SD, and usual range of makes.
workedExamples.solution: $\mu = 10 \times 0.8 = 8$. Var $= 10 \times 0.8 \times 0.2 = 1.6$; $\sigma = \sqrt{1.6} \approx 1.26$. Usual range: $8 \pm 2.5$, roughly $5.5$–$10$: nine makes is a shrug, four makes is news.

workedExamples.statement: A fair coin flipped $100$ times: mean, SD, and the usual range of heads.
workedExamples.solution: $\mu = 50$; Var $= 100 \times \frac{1}{4} = 25$; $\sigma = 5$. Usual: $50 \pm 10$, i.e., $40$–$60$ heads. Note $60$ heads in $100$ is ordinary while $6$ in $10$ (same 60%) is nothing at all — sample size sharpens judgment.

workedExamples.statement: Compare shapes: $n = 10$ with $p = 0.1$, $p = 0.5$, $p = 0.9$. Where does each pile its probability?
workedExamples.solution: $p = 0.1$: $\mu = 1$ — probability piles at low counts, long tail rightward (right-skew). $p = 0.5$: symmetric hill centered at $5$ (row 10 of Pascal's triangle, normalized). $p = 0.9$: mirror of the first, piled high with a left tail. Skew always points AWAY from the pile, toward the rare side.

commonMistakes.correction: np(1−p) is the VARIANCE; take the square root for σ. For n=100, p=½: Var = 25 but σ = 5 — mixing them misjudges 'usual' by a factor of five.
60% heads is meaningless alone: in 10 flips it's within one σ; in 1000 flips it's over 6σ — astonishing. Convert to counts and σ's before raising eyebrows.

tryIt.statement: For which $p$ does a binomial with fixed $n$ have the LARGEST spread — and why does that make sense?
tryIt.solution: $p = \frac{1}{2}$: Var $= np(1-p)$ maximizes at $p(1-p) = \frac{1}{4}$. Makes sense — a 50/50 trial is maximally unpredictable; near-certain trials ($p \to 0$ or $1$) barely vary.

steps.title: Atoms add
beats: One throw: mean $p$. Ten independent throws: a SUM of ten atoms.
Linearity (Unit 7): $E(X) = np$. Done.
Independence adds variances too: Var $= np(1-p)$.
$\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}$ — spread from anatomy, not tables.

steps.title: The pile knows its own centre
steps.teach: The marker sits at $\mu = np$ and the whiskers reach $\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}$ each way. Step $n$ and $p$ around: the peak tracks $np$ perfectly, the pile is symmetric at $p = \frac{1}{2}$, and it leans (skews) whenever $p$ moves off centre. You can **see** the formulas before you ever compute them.

steps.title: Mean and σ
steps.prompt: $n = 400$, $p = \frac{1}{4}$: mean and SD of the success count?
steps.explanation: $\mu = 400 \times \frac{1}{4} = 100$; Var $= 400 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{3}{4} = 75$, $\sigma = \sqrt{75}$. Forgetting the root (option 2) makes every outcome look 'usual'.

steps.title: Where the hill sits
beats: $p = \frac{1}{2}$: symmetric hill — literally Pascal's row, normalized (Unit 4!).
$p < \frac{1}{2}$: pile on low counts, tail stretching right.
$p > \frac{1}{2}$: the mirror image.
Bigger $n$: every hill smooths toward the bell. (Act Three is waiting.)

steps.title: Usual or newsworthy?
steps.prompt: A fair coin gives $62$ heads in $100$ flips. Verdict via $\mu \pm 2\sigma$?
options: Outside $50 \pm 10$ — mildly surprising, worth a second look
steps.explanation: $62$ sits just past $\mu + 2\sigma = 60$: eyebrow territory — rare-ish under fairness (a few percent), far from proof. The honest verdicts live on a scale, not a switch; next lesson sharpens the tool.

steps.title: Counts and σ's, not percents
steps.body: Headlines report percentages; judgment needs counts and σ's. '60% support' from 10 people is coin-flip noise; from 1000 it's a 6σ landslide. Before reacting to any rate, ask for n, convert to a count, and frame it with √(np(1−p)).

steps.title: The bell was born binomial
steps.body: De Moivre discovered the normal curve in 1733 as an APPROXIMATION — a shortcut for computing binomial probabilities at large n, where the exact chooses became unmanageable by hand. The most famous curve in statistics started life as a labor-saving device for exactly the tables you're building.

steps.title: What you learned
points: E = np, Var = np(1−p), σ by square root — from atom-anatomy.
Shape: symmetric at p = ½, skew points toward the rare side, n smooths.
Frame outcomes with μ ± 2σ; convert percents to counts first.

lessons.title: Simulation & Judging Surprise
lessons.concreteComparison: Your friend claims psychic powers: she called $8$ of $10$ coin flips. Convincing? Instead of debating, SIMULATE: flip $10$ fair coins, count the hits, repeat a thousand times, and see how often plain luck scores $8+$. The binomial answer is $\frac{56}{1024} \approx 5.5\%$ — luck does that one run in eighteen. Simulation is how you check models, judge claims, and answer questions too tangled for formulas.
lessons.objective: Design simulations of binomial experiments, and judge surprising claims by computing how often chance alone matches them.
concept: **Simulation** replaces analysis with repetition: model the chance mechanism faithfully (a digit $0$–$9$ per trial, digits $0$–$7$ meaning success simulates $p = 0.8$), run MANY trials, and read frequencies as probability estimates.

The **surprise recipe**: assume the boring explanation (fair coin, no powers, no improvement) — compute or simulate $P(\text{result at least this extreme})$ under that assumption. Small probability → the boring explanation strains; not-small → luck suffices. (Statistics will later name this a p-value; the logic is yours already.)

"At least this extreme" matters: the psychic's evidence is $8$ OR MORE hits — $P(X \ge 8) = \frac{56}{1024}$, not $P(X = 8)$ alone. Judging by the exact outcome overstates surprise (every exact outcome is rare).

Simulation earns trust by CALIBRATION: simulate something you can compute exactly (heads in $10$ flips), check the frequencies against the formula, THEN aim it at the tangled question. An unvalidated simulation is a random-number generator with confidence.
lessons.keyIdea: Model faithfully, repeat plenty, and judge claims by P(at least this extreme | boring explanation) — computed exactly when you can, simulated when you can't.

facts.title: The surprise recipe
facts.latex: P(\text{result at least this extreme} \mid \text{boring model})
facts.explanation: Small → the boring model strains; otherwise luck suffices.

facts.title: The psychic's number
facts.explanation: Tail probability, not the exact-outcome probability — extremes accumulate.

workedExamples.statement: Compute the psychic's tail exactly: $P(X \ge 8)$ for $n = 10$ fair-coin calls.
workedExamples.solution: $P(8) + P(9) + P(10) = \frac{\binom{10}{8} + \binom{10}{9} + \binom{10}{10}}{1024} = \frac{45 + 10 + 1}{1024} = \frac{56}{1024} \approx 0.055$. Luck alone does this about once in $18$ tries — suggestive, nowhere near proof. Demand a rerun.

workedExamples.statement: Design a simulation (random digits $0$–$9$) for: a $30\%$ free-throw shooter attempts $5$ shots; estimate $P(\text{at least } 3 \text{ makes})$. Then compute exactly.
workedExamples.solution: Model: one digit per shot, digits $\{0,1,2\}$ = make ($p = 0.3$). One trial = $5$ digits; count trials with $3+$ makes over many repetitions. Exact check: $P(3) + P(4) + P(5) = \binom{5}{3}(0.3)^3(0.7)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.3)^4(0.7) + (0.3)^5 = 0.1323 + 0.02835 + 0.00243 = 0.16308$.

workedExamples.statement: A factory's defect rate is supposedly $2\%$. Today's sample of $50$ contains $4$ defects. Frame the surprise computation — is the line drifting?
workedExamples.solution: Boring model: $p = 0.02$, $n = 50$, $\mu = 1$. Evidence: $4$ OR MORE defects. $P(X \ge 4) = 1 - [P(0) + P(1) + P(2) + P(3)]$ — with $\mu = 1$, four defects is far in the tail (about $1.8\%$). Verdict: unlikely under the boring model; stop the line and check. (One more sample before panic is also sound practice.)

commonMistakes.correction: Every exact outcome is individually rare — P(exactly 5 heads in 10) is only 24.6%! Surprise is measured by the TAIL: everything at least as extreme as the evidence.
A coin simulates 50%, nothing else. For p = 0.3 use digits {0,1,2} of 0–9; for p = 0.8 use {0–7}. The model must match the mechanism before repetition means anything.

tryIt.statement: A friend calls $5$ of $5$ coin flips. Compute the tail $P(X \ge 5)$ — impressed?
tryIt.solution: $P(X = 5) = \frac{1}{32} \approx 3.1\%$. Rarer than the psychic's $5.5\%$ — one more clean run would be genuinely hard to dismiss.

tryIt.statement: Design the digit-model for simulating a $70\%$ on-time bus over $7$ mornings.
tryIt.solution: One digit per morning; $\{0,\ldots,6\}$ = on time ($p = 0.7$), $\{7, 8, 9\}$ = late. One trial = $7$ digits; repeat many, read frequencies.

steps.title: Eight of ten
beats: "I'm psychic — I called 8 of 10 flips!"
Don't argue. Ask: how often does LUCK do that?
Simulate a thousand honest 10-flip rounds... or compute:
$P(X \ge 8) = \frac{56}{1024} \approx 5.5\%$. Once in 18. Suggestive; not proof.

steps.title: Judging surprise honestly
beats: 1 — Assume the BORING model (fair, unchanged, no powers).
2 — Compute/simulate $P(\text{at least this extreme})$ under it.
3 — Tiny → boring model strains. Not tiny → luck suffices.
The tail, not the exact outcome — extremes accumulate.

steps.title: Run the experiment many times
steps.teach: Each dot is one whole run — $n$ fair trials, plotted by its fraction of successes. Draw twenty runs at $n = 10$ and the pile is wide: getting 7 or 8 successes out of 10 is unremarkable. Switch to $n = 160$ and the same fraction would be shocking. Simulation gives you calibrated surprise: what's normal wobble, and what isn't.

steps.title: Why X ≥ 8?
steps.prompt: Why judge the psychic by $P(X \ge 8)$ instead of $P(X = 8)$?
options: 9 or 10 hits would've impressed us MORE — the evidence class is 'at least 8'
steps.explanation: "Surprising" means "this extreme or worse". Judging by exact outcomes makes everything look rare — P(exactly 5 of 10) is only 24.6% for the most ORDINARY result possible.

steps.title: Faithful digits
steps.prompt: To simulate a $p = 0.4$ event with random digits $0$–$9$, success should be…
steps.explanation: $p = 0.4$ needs exactly $4$ success-digits of $10$: $\{0,1,2,3\}$. Five digits simulates $0.5$; even digits also give $0.5$. The model IS the assignment — get it right before repeating.

steps.title: Calibrate before you trust
steps.body: First simulate something with a known answer (10 fair flips: the frequencies should track C(10,k)/1024). Once your simulation reproduces truth you can compute, point it at the question you can't. Skipping calibration is how confident nonsense gets made.

steps.title: The lady tasting tea
steps.body: Modern statistics' founding story is exactly this lesson: a colleague of R.A. Fisher claimed she could taste whether milk was poured before or after the tea. Fisher designed a randomized test — 8 cups, count the correct calls, compute the chance-alone tail. She got them all right (P = 1/70 under luck). The 'surprise recipe' has been standard science ever since.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Simulate: faithful model, many repetitions, frequencies as estimates — calibrated first.
Judge claims by the TAIL under the boring model.
Small tail strains the boring story; otherwise luck suffices. (Statistics will name this later.)

lessons.title: Capstone: The Binomial at Work
lessons.concreteComparison: One distribution, four industries in one afternoon: a clinic ("if the drug helps $60\%$ of patients, how likely is $9+$ helped of $12$?"), a call center ("$5\%$ of calls escalate — staff for how many?"), a game studio ("a $10\%$ drop rate — how many players get NOTHING in $20$ boxes?"), an examiner ("could a guesser pass this test?"). Same skeleton every time: verify BINS, set $n$ and $p$, choose the terms, frame with $\mu \pm 2\sigma$.
lessons.objective: Solve full-stack binomial problems end to end: verify the setting, compute the relevant terms, frame with mean/SD, and state honest conclusions.
concept: The complete workflow: (1) VERIFY the binomial setting (BINS — or note the approximation); (2) NAME $n$, $p$, and success; (3) TRANSLATE the question into a term list (exact, tail, or complement); (4) COMPUTE; (5) FRAME the answer with $\mu = np$ and $\sigma$; (6) CONCLUDE in the problem's own language.

Guessing on tests is the canonical guardrail computation: $20$ questions, $4$ options, pass at $12+$: $P(X \ge 12)$ with $p = 0.25$, $\mu = 5$ — the tail is astronomically small, which is WHY multiple-choice tests work at all.

Rare-event planning inverts the direction: with $p$ small and $n$ big ($5\%$ of $200$ calls), $\mu = 10$ and $\sigma \approx 3.1$ — staffing for $\mu + 2\sigma \approx 16$ covers the usual range; staffing for $\mu$ alone gets swamped every other day.

The binomial is also the bridge OUT of Act Two: a sample proportion is just $X/n$ — its behavior across samples (mean $p$, shrinking spread) is the seed of every poll's margin of error. Act Three grows it.
lessons.keyIdea: BINS → n, p, success → term list → compute → frame with μ ± 2σ → conclude in context. The same six steps run clinics, call centers, and exams.

facts.title: The workflow
facts.explanation: Six steps, every industry, no improvisation.

facts.title: Proportions preview
facts.explanation: Sample proportions are scaled binomials — Act Three's inference grows from here.

workedExamples.statement: A test has $20$ questions, $4$ options each, pass mark $12$. Show why guessing can't pass: compute $\mu$, $\sigma$, and locate $12$.
workedExamples.solution: $p = 0.25$: $\mu = 5$, Var $= 20 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 = 3.75$, $\sigma \approx 1.94$. The pass mark sits at $\frac{12 - 5}{1.94} \approx 3.6$ standard deviations above the mean — luck lives within $\pm 2$. Guessers fail by design, not by malice.

workedExamples.statement: A call center takes $200$ calls; $5\%$ escalate. How many escalations should staffing plan for?
workedExamples.solution: $\mu = 10$; $\sigma = \sqrt{200 \times 0.05 \times 0.95} = \sqrt{9.5} \approx 3.1$. Usual range: $10 \pm 6.2$ → plan for $\sim 16$ escalations to cover ordinary days; a $17+$ day is a genuine anomaly worth investigating, not a staffing failure.

workedExamples.statement: A loot box drops a rare item $10\%$ of the time. A player opens $20$ boxes. Find $P(\text{no rare item at all})$ — and the expected count.
workedExamples.solution: $P(X = 0) = 0.9^{20} \approx 0.122$ — one player in eight gets NOTHING despite $\mu = 2$ expected drops. Game designers publish the $10\%$; the $12\%$ empty-handed rate is what support tickets are made of. Both numbers are the same distribution.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting a probability without framing it.
commonMistakes.correction: "P = 0.122" floats; "about 1 in 8 players gets zero rares in 20 boxes, even though the average is 2" decides design questions. Steps 5–6 (frame, conclude) are the deliverable.
Half of days exceed the mean! Plan for μ + 2σ; treat beyond that as anomaly. The σ is why operations teams exist.

tryIt.statement: A true/false test ($p = 0.5$) has $10$ questions, pass at $8$. Compute the guessing tail $P(X \ge 8)$.
tryIt.solution: $\frac{45 + 10 + 1}{1024} = \frac{56}{1024} \approx 5.5\%$ — too lax for a real exam; hence more questions or higher marks.

tryIt.statement: An orchard's saplings survive transplanting with $p = 0.85$. Of $60$ planted, give the expected survivors and the usual range.

steps.title: One skeleton, four industries
beats: Clinic, call center, game studio, examiner — one afternoon.
Same six steps every time:
BINS → name $n, p$ → term list → compute → frame $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ → conclude.
Expertise is the workflow, not the formula.

steps.title: Plan for what?
steps.prompt: Escalations: $\mu = 10$, $\sigma \approx 3.1$. A manager staffs for exactly $10$. What happens?
options: Roughly half of days overflow — plan for $\mu + 2\sigma$ instead
steps.explanation: The mean is the CENTER: outcomes land above it about half the time. Planning for μ guarantees routine overload; μ + 2σ ≈ 16 covers the usual range and makes true anomalies visible.

steps.title: Run the six steps
steps.prompt: A vaccine trial: $12$ patients, drug helps each independently with claimed $p = 0.6$. NINE improved. The right surprise computation under the claim is…
options: $P(X \ge 9)$ with $n = 12, p = 0.6$ — the tail above the evidence
steps.explanation: Tail discipline (last lesson) inside the workflow: μ = 7.2, and 9+ is the evidence class. (The tail here is sizable — 9 of 12 is consistent with the claim, not proof beyond it.) Comparing raw percents skips the σ that makes 12 patients different from 1200.

steps.title: From counts to proportions
steps.body: Divide any binomial count by n and you get a sample PROPORTION — polls, conversion rates, survival rates. Its spread shrinks like 1/√n, which is why bigger samples pin the truth tighter. That one fact, grown carefully, is Act Three: turning data back into statements about the world.

steps.title: Loot boxes are regulated arithmetic
steps.body: Several countries now legally require games to publish drop rates — and regulators verify them by opening thousands of boxes and checking the observed counts against binomial tails, exactly your workflow. When a studio's '10%' produces suspiciously many empty streaks, the surprise recipe testifies in court.

steps.title: What you learned — Act Two complete
points: Six steps: BINS, (n,p), terms, compute, frame, conclude.
Plan for μ + 2σ; report tails, not floating decimals.
X/n is a proportion — Act Three: reading DATA honestly.

practice.statement: Is it binomial? A basketball player shoots until she makes $5$ baskets; $X = $ shots taken.
practice.solution: No — N fails: the trial count isn't fixed in advance (it depends on results).

practice.statement: A fair coin is flipped $5$ times. Find $P(\text{exactly } 4 \text{ heads})$.
A die is rolled $3$ times. Find $P(\text{exactly } 2 \text{ sixes})$.
A machine's parts are defective with $p = 0.05$. In a sample of $6$, find $P(\text{no defects})$ and $P(\text{at least one})$.
A $10$-question multiple-choice quiz has $5$ options each. A guesser needs at most... find $P(\text{exactly } 2 \text{ correct})$ with $p = 0.2$.
A fair coin flipped $64$ times: find $\mu$, $\sigma$, and the usual range of heads.
Design a digit-simulation ($0$–$9$) for a $p = 0.6$ free-throw shooter taking $8$ shots, and state the expected makes.
practice.solution: Digits $\{0,\ldots,5\}$ = make ($6$ of $10$); one trial $= 8$ digits. Expected: $8 \times 0.6 = 4.8$ makes.

testYourself.statement: Which are binomial settings? (a) counting sixes in $30$ die rolls; (b) counting hearts in $5$ cards dealt from one deck; (c) counting lefties in a random sample of $40$ students from a city of thousands.
testYourself.solution: (a) Yes — all BINS hold. (b) No — without replacement from $52$: dependent trials, drifting $p$. (c) Approximately — technically without replacement, but the sample is a tiny fraction of the population, so independence holds to excellent approximation.

testYourself.statement: A penalty-taker scores with $p = 0.7$. In $6$ kicks, find $P(\text{exactly } 4)$ and $P(\text{at least } 5)$.
A shipment passes if a $12$-item sample has at most $1$ defective. True defect rate: $8\%$. Find $P(\text{pass})$.
A fair die is rolled $180$ times. Frame the count of sixes with $\mu$ and $\sigma$, and judge: is $45$ sixes surprising?
testYourself.solution: $\mu = 30$; Var $= 180 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{5}{6} = 25$, $\sigma = 5$. $45$ sits $3\sigma$ above the mean — outside the usual range; genuinely surprising under fairness. Inspect the die (or the roller).

testYourself.statement: A game's claimed drop rate is $25\%$. A streamer opens $16$ boxes and gets ONE drop. Compute the tail $P(X \le 1)$ under the claim and give a verdict.
testYourself.solution: $\mu = 4$. $P(0) + P(1) = 0.75^{16} + 16 \times 0.25 \times 0.75^{15} \approx 0.010 + 0.053 = 0.063$ — about $6\%$: unlucky-but-plausible. One streamer's bad run doesn't convict the studio; a hundred streamers averaging low would.

testYourself.statement: An exam has $25$ questions, $5$ options each, pass mark $10$. Show with $\mu$, $\sigma$, and a $z$-style count that blind guessing is hopeless, then state the expected score of a student who truly knows $60\%$ of the material and guesses the rest.
testYourself.solution: Guesser: $\mu = 5$, Var $= 25 \times 0.2 \times 0.8 = 4$, $\sigma = 2$ — the pass mark is $\frac{10 - 5}{2} = 2.5\sigma$ up: rare. Mixed student: knows $15$ (all correct), guesses $10$ with $p = 0.2$ → expected $15 + 2 = 17$ — comfortably passing. Knowledge moves $\mu$; luck only rents $\sigma$.
